title: The Cross Bronx Expressway is completed
work: The Power Broker: Robert Moses and the Fall of New York (1974)
take: In the celebrated "One Mile" chapter, Caro argues that Robert Moses forced the Cross Bronx Expressway through the working-class, racially integrated East Tremont neighborhood rather than along a nearby alternate route that would have displaced far fewer people, destroying a stable community out of arrogance and inflexibility.

title: UN Secretariat tower opens on the East River
work: Manhattan Projects: The Rise and Fall of Urban Renewal in Cold War New York (2010)
take: Zipp reads postwar showpieces like the United Nations headquarters, Lincoln Center and Stuyvesant Town as a Cold War project to remake Manhattan as a symbol of American power, arguing that this renewal broke up diverse, disproportionately Black and Puerto Rican neighborhoods even as it produced the modern cityscape.

title: The Puerto Rican Great Migration peaks
work: From Colonia to Community: The History of Puerto Ricans in New York City (1983)
take: Sánchez Korrol traces how early twentieth-century Puerto Rican settlements, or colonias, grew into a defined New York community through their own organizational networks, mutual-aid institutions and the central role of women, revising earlier portrayals of migrants as disorganized newcomers.

title: The Ocean Hill-Brownsville teachers' strike
work: The Strike That Changed New York: Blacks, Whites, and the Ocean Hill-Brownsville Crisis (2004)
take: Podair argues that the 1968 school-control conflict was the most racially divisive event in New York in a century, shattering the old Black-Jewish liberal alliance and helping reorganize the city's politics along racial lines for decades afterward.

title: Hip-hop is born in the Bronx
work: Can't Stop Won't Stop: A History of the Hip-Hop Generation (2005)
take: Chang argues that hip-hop emerged from the deindustrialization, disinvestment and gang culture of the 1970s Bronx and became the defining expression of a multiracial post-civil-rights generation, turning conditions of urban abandonment into a global cultural movement.

title: 'Ford to City: Drop Dead'
work: Fear City: New York's Fiscal Crisis and the Rise of Austerity Politics (2017)
take: Phillips-Fein argues that the 1975 fiscal crisis was seized upon by bankers and officials to impose austerity, empower unelected control boards over the city's finances, and discredit New York's social-democratic experiment, making the city an early laboratory for a politics of cutbacks that later spread nationally.

title: The blackout that broke the city's spirit
work: Ladies and Gentlemen, the Bronx Is Burning: 1977, Baseball, Politics, and the Battle for the Soul of a City (2005)
take: Mahler braids the 1977 blackout and looting, the Son of Sam manhunt, the Koch-Cuomo mayoral race and the warring Yankees into a single portrait of a city at its nadir, arguing that 1977 was a battle for the soul of New York during its deepest crisis.

title: The AIDS epidemic surfaces in New York
work: The Gentrification of the Mind: Witness to a Lost Imagination (2012)
take: Schulman argues that the mass death of the AIDS epidemic emptied out Lower East Side and other bohemian neighborhoods and cleared the way for their gentrification, and that the unacknowledged trauma produced a broader cultural conformity she calls a gentrification of the imagination.

title: The crack epidemic engulfs the city
who: Craig Reinarman and Harry G. Levine
work: Crack in America: Demon Drugs and Social Justice (1997)
take: Reinarman and Levine argue that the crack "epidemic" was amplified into a moral panic by politicians and media in the late 1980s, and that this drug scare helped justify harsh sentencing and enforcement policies whose burden fell disproportionately on poor Black and Latino communities.

title: Times Square is reborn
work: The Invention of Brownstone Brooklyn: Gentrification and the Search for Authenticity in Postwar New York (2011)
take: Osman traces gentrification to a 1960s-70s grassroots movement of middle-class "brownstoners" searching for authenticity against suburban conformity and technocratic urban renewal, arguing that this romantic revaluation of the old city, not just banks and developers, set the stage for later waves of upscaling.

title: The police killing of Amadou Diallo
work: Spatial Regulation in New York City: From Urban Renewal to Zero Tolerance (2011)
take: Chronopoulos argues that Giuliani-era "quality-of-life" and zero-tolerance policing was part of a longer project of spatial regulation aimed at clearing disorder to enable real-estate investment and tourism, a project that eroded civil rights and fell hardest on poor and minority New Yorkers.

title: CompStat and the great crime decline
work: The City That Became Safe: New York's Lessons for Urban Crime and Its Control (2011)
take: Zimring documents New York's roughly 80 percent crime drop from 1990 to 2009 and argues it happened without mass imprisonment or major demographic change, crediting a mix of more police, targeted tactics like CompStat and hot-spot policing, while cautioning that "broken windows" zero-tolerance was never a consistent policy and cannot claim sole credit.

title: 9/11 Memorial opens at Ground Zero
work: Tourists of History: Memory, Kitsch, and Consumerism from Oklahoma City to Ground Zero (2007)
take: Sturken argues that Americans processed the trauma of 9/11 through consumerism, kitsch commemoration and tourism at sites like Ground Zero, a mode of memory that preserved a comforting sense of national innocence while easing acquiescence to expanded security and military policies.

title: Occupy Wall Street takes Zuccotti Park
work: The Democracy Project: A History, a Crisis, a Movement (2013)
take: Graeber, an anthropologist and participant, argues that Occupy's power lay less in the "99 percent" slogan he helped popularize than in its anarchist-inflected practice of leaderless general assemblies and consensus decision-making, a prefigurative model that refused the legitimacy of existing political and financial institutions.

title: Containerization and the decline of the port
work: Working-Class New York: Life and Labor Since World War II (2000)
take: Freeman argues that unionized manufacturing and waterfront jobs underpinned a distinctive postwar social-democratic New York of public housing, cheap transit and free CUNY, and that deindustrialization, including the loss of the docks to containerized New Jersey ports, gutted that working-class base and the political order it sustained.

title: The Stonewall uprising ignites gay liberation
take: Duberman reconstructs the 1969 uprising through the lives of six participants and argues that Stonewall marked a generational, organizational and ideological shift that transformed a cautious homophile cause into a militant gay-liberation movement embedded in the era's broader social protest.
